title: Where Is the SNAP Enrollment Gap?
text:
This is a reworked version of my earlier SNAP visualization. In the previous post, I looked at household size and children share across poverty quartiles — but those variables turned out not to be significant predictors of enrollment gaps. This visualization corrects that by asking a better question:
Which census tracts are enrolling fewer people than their poverty level predicts, and does this problem exist across all poverty levels or only in the poorest tracts?

title: Where Is the SNAP Enrollment Gap?
Where Is the SNAP Enrollment Gap?
text:
How to Read This Plot
The plot is split into four panels from left to right based on poverty level — Q1 is the least poor and Q4 is the most poor.

X axis — the number of people living at or below 125% of the poverty line in that census tract (log scale so small and large tracts are both visible)
Y axis — the combined SNAP and MFIP take-up rate. Higher means more eligible people are actually enrolling
Dot color — red tracts are enrolling fewer people than a regression model predicts given their poverty level. Blue tracts are enrolling at or above expectations
Panels — splitting by poverty quartile lets us see whether underenrollment is only a high-poverty problem or whether it shows up everywhere

title: Where Is the SNAP Enrollment Gap?
section: What Changed From the Previous Version
text:
What Changed From the Previous Version
In my earlier post (01_my-second-code) I visualized household size and children share across the same four poverty quartiles. However, those variables were tested in a regression model and found to be statistically insignificant (p = 0.770 and p = 0.539 respectively). This reworked version instead uses a regression-based enrollment gap to correctly identify which tracts are underenrolling relative to what their poverty level would predict.

title: Where Is the SNAP Enrollment Gap?
text:
Key Finding
Underenrollment is not just a high-poverty problem — red tracts appear in every quartile from Q1 to Q4. This means that even in neighborhoods with relatively low poverty, some tracts are still not enrolling as many people as expected. The problem is widespread across Hennepin County and cannot be solved by targeting only the poorest neighborhoods.

title: SNAP Household Size & Children by Poverty Level
text:
This visualization explores how household size and children share relate to SNAP enrollment across Hennepin County neighborhoods in 2024. We split neighborhoods into four poverty groups to see how these patterns change as poverty increases.
The key question we are trying to answer is:
Are larger and poorer families actually enrolling in SNAP at the rates we would expect?

title: SNAP Household Size & Children by Poverty Level
text:
This visualization explores how household size and children share relate to SNAP enrollment across Hennepin County neighborhoods in 2024. We split neighborhoods into four poverty groups to see how these patterns change as poverty increases.
The key question we are trying to answer is:
Are larger and poorer families actually enrolling in SNAP at the rates we would expect?

title: SNAP Household Size & Children by Poverty Level
SNAP Household Size & Children by Poverty Level
text:
How to Read This Plot
The plot is split into four panels from left to right based on poverty level — Q1 is the least poor and Q4 is the most poor.

X axis — what percentage of SNAP recipients in that neighborhood are children. Higher means more kids are getting SNAP
Y axis — average household size. Higher means bigger families are on SNAP
Dashed line — the county average household size of 2.27 people per household
Dot color — the SNAP take-up rate. Light blue means few eligible people are enrolling, while orange and red means most eligible people are enrolling
Dot size — how many SNAP cases are in that neighborhood

title: SNAP Household Size & Children by Poverty Level
text:
Key Finding
As poverty increases from Q1 to Q4, households get larger and have more children. However, the dots in Q4 are mostly blue — meaning even the poorest neighborhoods with the biggest families are not enrolling at the rates we would expect. This suggests that poverty and family size alone do not guarantee SNAP enrollment, and there may be other barriers keeping eligible families from accessing the program.

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Welcome to my Data Visualization Portfolio! I’m Armand, a senior at Augsburg University studying Computer Science with minors in Data Science and Mathematics.
This blog will showcase my work in data visualization, exploring how data-driven approaches can be applied to real-world challenges. Stay tuned for projects highlighting everything from exploratory data analysis to interactive visualizations.
Optional: You can publish this to your own github, although it does take some work. Some information is found here: LINK

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.

title: Armand Bashige
text: I am a senior at Augsburg University pursuing a Bachelor’s degree in Computer Science with minors in Data Science and Mathematics. I am passionate about leveraging my problem-solving skills to develop innovative solutions that create meaningful impact. With a strong foundation in Python and Java, I have hands-on experience implementing data structures, algorithms, and core software development principles. I am particularly interested in building efficient, scalable systems and applying data-driven approaches to real-world challenges.

title: Median Household Income in Hennepin County
text: This visualization explores the distribution of median household income across zip codes in Hennepin County, Minnesota. Using a choropleth map, we can see how income levels vary geographically throughout the county.

title: Median Household Income in Hennepin County
text: This visualization explores the distribution of median household income across zip codes in Hennepin County, Minnesota. Using a choropleth map, we can see how income levels vary geographically throughout the county.

title: Median Household Income in Hennepin County
text:
Key Takeaways
The map reveals notable income disparities across Hennepin County. Western suburbs tend to show higher median household incomes, while areas closer to downtown Minneapolis show more variation. This type of geographic visualization helps highlight spatial patterns in economic data that might not be obvious from tables or summary statistics alone.
